skill.name: azure-kusto
skill.description: Query and analyze Azure Data Explorer data with KQL. This skill helps inspect Kusto clusters, databases, schemas, logs, telemetry, and time series data.
skill.summary: Run KQL analysis for Azure Data Explorer logs, telemetry, schemas, and time series data.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance indicators, but manual review confirms most are false positives caused by KQL examples, the ADX abbreviation, and KQL operator names. The real risk is moderate: the skill documents Azure CLI and Kusto REST fallbacks that execute external commands and send queries to Azure services using user credentials. No prompt injection attempt, malware intent, credential theft, or hidden data exfiltration evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: External Azure CLI and Kusto REST Fallback
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to run Azure CLI commands and an az rest call against a Kusto endpoint if MCP tools fail. This is legitimate Azure Data Explorer functionality, but it can execute external commands and submit queries to Azure using the user's current credentials, so users should review cluster, database, and query parameters before running fallback commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically tied to Azure Kusto operations, not hidden execution. Risk remains because they invoke az commands and post query bodies to a remote Kusto service with user-provided parameters.

low_findings.title: KQL Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most static external command hits occur inside fenced KQL examples or inline KQL function names. These are documentation examples for querying Azure Data Explorer and are not Ruby backtick execution or shell command substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are KQL code fences, KQL operators, or inline function names such as ago, bin, summarize, and percentiles. No Ruby execution context exists in the skill file.

low_findings.title: ADX Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts map to descriptive Azure Data Explorer text and the ADX abbreviation. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic operation is present at the reported locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows Azure service descriptions and KQL ordering syntax, not crypto APIs or algorithm use. The static match is lexical rather than semantic.

low_findings.title: Templated Kusto Service URL
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes a hardcoded URL template for the official Kusto REST query endpoint. This network destination is expected for Azure Data Explorer usage, but query contents may contain sensitive operational data and should be scoped to approved clusters.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL template clearly targets a Kusto service endpoint and is part of documented Azure fallback behavior. It is not suspicious by itself, but it confirms network interaction.

low_findings.title: KQL Take Operator Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The system reconnaissance alert maps to the KQL take operator used to limit query output. It does not inspect the host system, enumerate files, or collect local environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a best practice about limiting Azure Data Explorer result sets. There is no local system command or reconnaissance behavior at that location.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Azure CLI and Kusto REST Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to run Azure CLI commands and an az rest call against a Kusto endpoint if MCP tools fail. This is legitimate Azure Data Explorer functionality, but it can execute external commands and submit queries to Azure using the user's current credentials, so users should review cluster, database, and query parameters before running fallback commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically tied to Azure Kusto operations, not hidden execution. Risk remains because they invoke az commands and post query bodies to a remote Kusto service with user-provided parameters.

dangerous_patterns.title: Templated Kusto Service URL
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes a hardcoded URL template for the official Kusto REST query endpoint. This network destination is expected for Azure Data Explorer usage, but query contents may contain sensitive operational data and should be scoped to approved clusters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL template clearly targets a Kusto service endpoint and is part of documented Azure fallback behavior. It is not suspicious by itself, but it confirms network interaction.

content.user_title: Query Azure Data Explorer with KQL
content.value_statement: Azure telemetry and log data can be hard to explore without the right KQL patterns. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Kusto schema discovery, query design, and safe Azure fallback commands.
actual_capabilities: Guides KQL query creation for Azure Data Explorer tables and databases.
Supports schema discovery with Kusto table and database inspection tools.
Provides query patterns for filtering, aggregation, joins, and time series analysis.
Documents Azure MCP tools for listing clusters, databases, schemas, and query execution.
Provides Azure CLI and Kusto REST fallback commands when MCP tools are unavailable.
limitations: Requires valid Azure credentials and permissions for target Kusto resources.
Does not replace database access controls or approval workflows.
Fallback commands depend on Azure CLI availability and correct tenant context.
Query quality depends on accurate table names, schema details, and time filters.

use_cases.title: Investigate Application Logs
use_cases.description: Build focused KQL queries that filter recent events, group errors, and identify high-impact services.

use_cases.title: Analyze Device Telemetry
use_cases.description: Explore IoT telemetry with time buckets, percentiles, and trend summaries for operational monitoring.

use_cases.title: Review Security Events
use_cases.description: Correlate Kusto tables with joins and severity filters to support threat investigation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Find Recent Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Azure Kusto to inspect recent records in my table. Ask for the cluster, database, table, and time range if missing.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Errors by Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a KQL query that counts errors by service and one-hour time bucket. Include a safe limit and explain each step.

prompt_templates.title: Correlate Tables for Root Cause
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me join event and log tables by correlation identifier, filter critical failures, and project the fields needed for triage.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Slow KQL Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my KQL query for performance. Recommend earlier filters, smaller projections, time limits, and safer fallback commands if needed.

output_examples.input: Show the last hour of failed sign-in events and summarize them by result type.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for the target cluster, database, and table if missing, then returns a time-bounded KQL plan with result grouping and a short explanation of expected columns.

output_examples.input: My telemetry query is timing out. Help me improve it.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends adding a narrow time range, filtering before joins, projecting only needed fields, and using a small sample before running the full query.

output_examples.input: List the databases in my Kusto cluster when the MCP tool is unavailable.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes the Azure CLI fallback, identifies required parameters, and reminds the user to verify subscription, resource group, cluster, and tenant context.

best_practices: Start every exploratory query with a clear time range and result limit.
Inspect table schemas before writing joins, projections, or aggregations.
Review Azure CLI fallback commands before execution, especially cluster, database, and tenant values.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad queries across long time ranges without filters.
Do not paste sensitive query results into prompts unless they are approved for analysis.
Do not use fallback REST commands without confirming the target Azure tenant and cluster.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps create and run KQL workflows for Azure Data Explorer logs, telemetry, schemas, and time series data.

faq.question: Does it work with SQL databases?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on Kusto Query Language and Azure Data Explorer resources, not relational SQL databases.

faq.question: Does it need Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. Query execution and resource discovery require Azure credentials with permission to access the target Kusto resources.

faq.question: Can it use Azure CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents Azure CLI fallback commands for cases where MCP Kusto tools fail or are unavailable.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes legitimate external commands and network calls to Azure services. Users should verify parameters before running them.

faq.question: Can it help optimize KQL performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for time filters, projections, aggregations, materialized views, and result limits.
